article_meta.title: Witness Giving Oath During TrialgettyWomen are not believed, one legal whitepaper finds

reasoning: The model does a generally good job identifying author-level bias and avoiding quoted text. All three flagged instances come from the author's own prose or structural choices. The bias type labels are reasonable. There are some minor issues: the Slant item is more accurately Bias by Omission (since no counterargument appears anywhere in the article, not just de-emphasized), and a few additional instances of author bias could have been flagged.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: All three flagged items come from the author's own prose or structural analysis. No quoted text was flagged as author bias. The model correctly excluded Joan Meier's opinions, the Women in Courts study quotes, and the misperceptions list.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: All three items are defensible. The Depp-Heard interpretation is genuinely the author presenting a contested opinion as fact. The structural slant observation is valid. The 'force a confrontation' word choice is a reasonable flag. The second item describes structural absence rather than quoting specific text, which is appropriate for omission/slant analysis.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model missed several author-level bias instances: 'women are not believed' in the headline presented as fact rather than a finding; 'No matter the content of her story, women are considered unreliable narrators of their own experiences' is a sweeping opinion presented as fact; 'the abuses perpetrated on women and the struggles they face coming forward to tell their truths' uses loaded language and scare quotes around 'truths'; 'unprecedented wave' is a subjective qualifying adjective; the fiction reference (Woman in the Window, Girl on the Train) used as evidence of systemic bias is arguably flawed logic.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: The Depp-Heard item as 'Opinion Statements Presented as Fact' is well-applied. The structural observation is labeled 'Slant' but given no counterargument appears anywhere in the article, 'Bias by Omission' would be more accurate per the definitions provided. 'Word Choice' for 'force a confrontation' is reasonable. So one label is debatably wrong.
